title: How to Import and Analyze Phyphox CSV Data in RStudio
text:
The phyphox app is a really cool way to collect your own data. You can read more about it here: phyphox
library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.1.4     ✔ readr     2.1.6
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.1     ✔ tibble    3.3.0
✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.0     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

title: How to Import and Analyze Phyphox CSV Data in RStudio
text:
GPS Safety
GPS data sets have precise longitude and latitude values. Share raw data cautiously to avoid leaking private data. (note: this is also useful for geometry. The earth can be seen as a sphere and this data can be converted spherical coordinates)

title: How to Import and Analyze Phyphox CSV Data in RStudio
How to Import and Analyze Phyphox CSV Data in RStudio
text:
Linear Equations
Here is the fun part: we can use calculus and linear geometry to analyse this data.

ggplot(data=walk, aes(x=`Time (s)`, y=`Distance (km)`, color=`Direction (°)`)) +
  geom_point() +
  theme_gray()

Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









Above you can see that distance is approximately a line. This means we can use the equation \[y=mx+b\] to help us analyse it. After observing the graph we can tell my walk was at roughly a constant speed and direction.

ggplot(data=walk, aes(x=`Time (s)`, y=`Distance (km)`)) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm") +
  theme_gray()

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'


Warning: Removed 1 row containing non-finite outside the scale range
(`stat_smooth()`).









Now, how do we get the slope of this line? Well, rcode can do this for us. You can also do it the old fashion way if you want.

summary(lm(`Distance (km)` ~ `Time (s)`, data=walk))


Call:
lm(formula = `Distance (km)` ~ `Time (s)`, data = walk)

Residuals:
       Min         1Q     Median         3Q        Max 
-0.0138009 -0.0021663  0.0009866  0.0040016  0.0176067 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept) 9.974e-03  5.054e-04   19.74   <2e-16 ***
`Time (s)`  1.673e-03  1.890e-06  885.54   <2e-16 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.005417 on 460 degrees of freedom
  (1 observation deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.9994,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.9994 
F-statistic: 7.842e+05 on 1 and 460 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16



ggplot(data=walk, aes(x=`Time (s)`, y=`Altitude (m)`, color=`Vertical Accuracy (m)`)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method="lm")

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'


Warning: The following aesthetics were dropped during statistical transformation:
colour.
ℹ This can happen when ggplot fails to infer the correct grouping structure in
  the data.
ℹ Did you forget to specify a `group` aesthetic or to convert a numerical
  variable into a factor?

title: About
text: This is a simple blog page made to share my passion for projects on computers. I am happy to finally get a public interface and plan on doing a lot of fun posts.

title: The server admin
text:
Lou-Lou is an amazing little American Bulldog who is energetic and quick to action. If the server goes down she’s on the case.

Nala is the cutest elder min-pin around with 15 years under her belt, she gives the orders around here.

title: Collecting Listening Data with Apple Music
text:
This requires a mac of some type.


Create a new playlist with the songs or album you want data from.










Note



Choose plain text unicode will not work for imports


    # Analysis of apple music with R

library(tidyverse)

── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
✔ dplyr     1.2.0     ✔ readr     2.2.0
✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
✔ ggplot2   4.0.2     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
✔ lubridate 1.9.5     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
✔ purrr     1.2.1     
── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors

library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(moderndive)

ghost <- read_tsv("skeleta.txt")

Rows: 10 Columns: 31
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: "\t"
chr (11): Name, Artist, Composer, Album, Genre, Date Modified, Date Added, K...
dbl (11): Size, Time, Disc Number, Disc Count, Track Number, Track Count, Ye...
lgl  (9): Grouping, Work, Movement Number, Movement Count, Movement Name, Vo...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

ghost |> 
  head()

# A tibble: 6 × 31
  Name   Artist Composer Album Grouping Work  `Movement Number` `Movement Count`
  <chr>  <chr>  <chr>    <chr> <lgl>    <lgl> <lgl>             <lgl>           
1 Peace… Ghost  A Ghoul… Skel… NA       NA    NA                NA              
2 Lachr… Ghost  A Ghoul… Skel… NA       NA    NA                NA              
3 Satan… Ghost  A Ghoul… Skel… NA       NA    NA                NA              
4 Guidi… Ghost  A Ghoul… Skel… NA       NA    NA                NA              
5 De Pr… Ghost  A Ghoul… Skel… NA       NA    NA                NA              
6 Cenot… Ghost  A Ghoul… Skel… NA       NA    NA                NA              
# ℹ 23 more variables: `Movement Name` <lgl>, Genre <chr>, Size <dbl>,
#   Time <dbl>, `Disc Number` <dbl>, `Disc Count` <dbl>, `Track Number` <dbl>,
#   `Track Count` <dbl>, Year <dbl>, `Date Modified` <chr>, `Date Added` <chr>,
#   `Bit Rate` <dbl>, `Sample Rate` <dbl>, `Volume Adjustment` <lgl>,
#   Kind <chr>, Equalizer <lgl>, Comments <lgl>, Plays <dbl>,
#   `Last Played` <chr>, Skips <dbl>, `Last Skipped` <chr>, `My Rating` <lgl>,
#   Location <chr>






ggplot(data=ghost, aes(x=`Time`/60,y=`Plays`,color=`Name`)) +
  geom_point(size=3) +
  geom_smooth(method="lm", se=FALSE, color="black") +
  theme_minimal()

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'










ghost <- ghost |> 
  mutate(minutes=`Time`/60)

ghost_mod <- lm(Plays ~ minutes, data=ghost) 
ghost_mod |> 
  get_regression_table()

# A tibble: 2 × 7
  term      estimate std_error statistic p_value lower_ci upper_ci
  <chr>        <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>
1 intercept   32.9        52.4     0.628   0.547    -87.9    154. 
2 minutes      0.966      11.1     0.087   0.933    -24.5     26.5

ghost_mod |> 
  get_regression_summaries() 

# A tibble: 1 × 9
  r_squared adj_r_squared   mse  rmse sigma statistic p_value    df  nobs
      <dbl>         <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1     0.001        -0.124  592.  24.3  27.2     0.008   0.933     1    10

title: Welcome To ross2006.org and sugerplum.uk
text:
Sugarplum: The American Pitbull


Pictured above is the source of the domain sugerplum.uk
Welcome, this is my server. I am Aiden Ross, a student of computer science and mathematics at Appalachian State University. At the time of writing this I have been working on and off on this server for about ~1.5 months.

title: Welcome To ross2006.org and sugerplum.uk
text:
Purpose
This has been a project for education, personal utility, and fun. Currently, I have quickly expanded to hosting many cloud alternatives.

title: Blog Code Style Guide
text: This page is intended to be a living document. As preferences changes and I become more informed I may update certain things. Roughly, this is supposed to be a place to show the structure to expect and more importantly to keep me consistent.

title: Blog Code Style Guide
text:
single char names
Often, rcode is used for quick computations; As long as the value is not important beyond a single chunk I will allow the use of single character variable names.

# simplistic for short code 

x <- 10
x 

[1] 10

title: Blog Code Style Guide
text:
snake case
Snake case is usually clear and easy to read for short blocks of code.

# be specific given some context
some_variable <- 1203

some_variable

[1] 1203

title: Blog Code Style Guide
What can a Tegra K1 do in 2026?
text:
Overview
First, I have attached an image of the board below.



The TEGRA K1 board.


At the time this little board pushed a full desktop architecture for its GPU. (NVIDIA 2013)

Tegra K1

Cortex A15 4-PLUS-1 processor
Keplar GPU with 192 Cuda cores
2 GB ram
Support for opengl 4.4
Support for CUDA 6.6
Support for DirectX 11




Gaming
Nvidia with this board claimed it was more powerful than the xbox360 and ps3(without accounting for spus) (see TegraWhitePages?). I would like to test this claim; I will account for the overhead in processing for arm and linux compatibility from windows titles.





References

NVIDIA. 2013. “Whitepaper-NVIDIA Tegra K1-a New Era in Mobile Computing.” 2013. https://www.nvidia.com/content/PDF/tegra_white_papers/tegra-K1-whitepaper.pdf.

title: Ghost Listening Report
text:
Data Analysis
Import any needed libraries.

library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(moderndive)

As usual I import the data from my apple music.

ghost <- read_tsv("Ghost.txt", show_col_types = FALSE)

# cleanup data
ghost <- ghost |> 
    janitor::clean_names() |> 
    janitor::remove_empty() |> 
    filter(!is.na(plays), plays > 0) |> 
    filter(!is.na(duration), !is.na(plays)) |> 
    rename(duration = time)

value for "which" not specified, defaulting to c("rows", "cols")


Warning: There was 1 warning in `filter()`.
ℹ In argument: `!is.na(duration)`.
Caused by warning in `is.na()`:
! is.na() applied to non-(list or vector) of type 'closure'

ghost |> 
  head()

# A tibble: 6 × 22
  name        artist composer album genre   size duration disc_number disc_count
  <chr>       <chr>  <chr>    <chr> <chr>  <dbl>    <dbl>       <dbl>      <dbl>
1 Imperium    Ghost  A Ghoul… IMPE… Metal 4.10e6      100           1          1
2 Kaisarion   Ghost  A Ghoul… IMPE… Metal 1.08e7      302           1          1
3 Spillways   Ghost  A Ghoul… IMPE… Metal 7.46e6      196           1          1
4 Call Me Li… Ghost  A Ghoul… IMPE… Metal 1.07e7      284           1          1
5 Hunter’s M… Ghost  A Ghoul… IMPE… Metal 7.58e6      196           1          1
6 Watcher In… Ghost  A Ghoul… IMPE… Metal 1.27e7      348           1          1
# ℹ 13 more variables: track_number <dbl>, track_count <dbl>, year <dbl>,
#   date_modified <chr>, date_added <chr>, bit_rate <dbl>, sample_rate <dbl>,
#   kind <chr>, plays <dbl>, last_played <chr>, skips <dbl>,
#   last_skipped <chr>, location <chr>


Plot the data in a histogram.

ggplot(ghost, aes(x=plays, fill=album)) +
    geom_histogram(binwidth=5, na.rm = TRUE)














Note



The data is significantly right-skewed, therefore, the median will likely be more accurate here.


Next, I create a table of some useful values.

ghost <- ghost |> 
  mutate(total_seconds = duration * plays)
ghost |>
  summarise(
    meadian_plays = median(plays),
    mean_plays = mean(plays),
    total_plays = sum(plays),
    total_hours = sum(total_seconds, na.rm = TRUE) / 3600
  ) |> 
  kable()




meadian_plays
mean_plays
total_plays
total_hours




5
27.91525
1647
117.8828
